speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is August the 5th, 1996. And we are conducting an interview with Rachel Drabkin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a message that you'd like to give to future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I would like the future generation, not only Jewish, you know, the whole world to know and remember and never forget what we went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think we can help to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: To tell everybody. That's what I give today, to keep the old, second and [INAUDIBLE] and another generation of kids to look at that and never forget about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you talk with your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I talk with them. I talk with them. They learn there at school, too, a lot of this, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they ask you many questions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They ask me some questions, like from my mother and father because they never know them. And but my sister what's happened. Then I-- and I answer them, you know? But they still-- it's-- they have to know more about it. They don't know a little-- every little thing like I tell you now here. They should get in the future and watch it and see it what everybody when through, not only me, everybody from the Holocaust survive. Everybody went out this completely difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mrs. Drabkin. We--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: --appreciate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Drabkin, would you like to introduce us to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the left is my husband, Sam. And in front for me is Rebecca. And on the right in the front is Jessica. On the right in the chair is Simon. Behind me is my son, Aric. And on the left side is my daughter-in-law, Chedva.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Drabkin, would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: After all, what we've been through, the all the years, we be very lucky we come to Australia in this country. And I built there a family. My family from on the-- here is three children-- three grandchildren, on which I've got another two. They're not here. They're at work. And my [INAUDIBLE]. Unfortunately, I lost my daughter. And I won another daughter. That's my daughter-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Chedva, and you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's amazing to hear the stories that-- the real-life stories that-- that they went through, through the camps, because my-- my own mother is fairly private about the experiences that she went through herself and doesn't like to talk about them. That's not her way of dealing with it, which probably hasn't done her too well through life, I suppose. She-- she finds it very difficult to deal with-- with that part of her life, whereas Sam and Rachel are very-- very open about it. So I've been able to hear all their experiences and relay them to the children, whereas my mother is-- isn't happy about relaying those experiences. So it's quite amazing to hear the-- the stories that they have-- that they've been through.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Aric?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think it's amazing that we're the third generation here that-- has firsthand experience to hear from a survivor, which is a-- it's a shame for the next generation who will never have a first-hand experience. And that's why this is so important, because as much as you can read the stories, to hear first-hand, that's very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I think, also, it's-- I was speaking to somebody today, and they were saying-- And I asked him how to pick up his life. And he said to me that he reached out on sport. And I said, what do you mean sport? And he said, well, his parents were so busy trying to build a new life, coming back from such a trauma that his father never took him to football over the years.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said it was such a minor thing. But it made me realize, too, that we never really did a lot together. And that was because they were so engrossed in rebuilding their lives. And you never-- you never sort of look down upon it for whatever-- sort of feel bad it. But just-- it's a fact of life. And it's what happened to me today.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I suppose that's how we suffered, I think. Yeah, I guess that didn't spend enough time with our parents as much as our kids are now spending with us. So I-- I think we're fortunate to see that these-- my kids can see what actually happened, what-- what they've gained. What they've got now is what I didn't have, even though it's not-- even though what I had is much more than what they had. So-- And Jessica, what about your roots project?

speaker: interviewee
text: At school, I did a Roots project, which we choose generally a family. And I interviewed my bubbe and zayde. And they were telling me all-- all stories about what they went through in the war and with the ghettos and the concentration camps. And I learned a lot from them. And I hope they can tell me more stories. And I'm happy that I did the Roots project, because I know that I'll always have what they told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Simon?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm happy that they-- they're not in the war anymore and they survived. And my-- my zayde, he had a star. And he-- he-- he showed it to Jessica's class in grade three. And everyone said how-- how good it is that he still has it. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the identification tag from the Vilna ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you show your classmates too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you enjoy talking with Bubbe and Zayde--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and learning?

speaker: interviewee
text: I like, like, hearing what they've been through and that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rebecca? What do you talk about with Bubbe and Zayde?

speaker: interviewee
text: About when they got their hair shaved off and how-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and how they had to wear patches on their clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else did they talk-- tell you about? Did they tell you about their early life before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. During the war, Bubbe had a little photo of Zayde under her tongue when he-- they was looking around and making sure that they didn't have anything on them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you-- it's very special to keep that for such a long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, all.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the front from the two girls, one on the right one, her name is Alpke Schlofsky. And on the left, the girl was in the front behind the girls, I am. I'm Rachel there. And then other two, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this photo was taken in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where about?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Vilna in a forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could be a bit larger too. I think you might--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the front in the middle of the picture, this I, myself, Rachel. In the back side is on the left, there used to be my boyfriend. His name is [NON-ENGLISH]. And then other one is Breshka. And in the back one, my boyfriend on the left side, is one is Eitla Maleb. The other one is Alpke Schlofsky. And then is a boy standing here is Berke Shesken. And then is another girlfriend mine is Mashke Gordon. And the last, the middle one, I don't remember. And then the last girl here on the right is [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And where--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was done in 19-- I think in 1938 at school.

speaker: interviewer
text: They're all school friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was all school friends. There was a-- a end of the year, there was a party. And then we went all together to the party. Rachel-- not-- yeah? That's me, Rachel. The picture is done after the war in 1946 in Vilna. That's my husband. He-- the picture was taken in Vilna, 1946 after the war. This is a picture what was taking a group from my husband with a group of friends in 1939. And when the war broke up, I took this with me to the concentration camp. And it was too big to hold it all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I just cutted up his head. And I keeped it with me. So people will ask, how can you bring him through? So I hide him, my ring and my husband's picture right under my tongue. When I went through all the concentration camps, and I bring them back in Vilna. And we make from them a bigger picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] This is a picture for my grandchildren. On the left is Charlie. He's 25 years old. He's a son from my late daughter. And the next one is Jessica. She's 13 years old. And this is a daughter from my son. And this is Darren. He's 22 years old. He's a-- a son from my late daughter. And this is Rebecca and Simon are twins, what when they're 10 years old, from my son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at the back?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the back, my husband. How can I forget something like that? That's my late daughter. She passed away in 1985. And she have two sons, Charlie and Darren. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her name is Pauline Kol. This is a monument for this is put in, in Ponary, where my mother and my mother-in-law. They were shot there through the Germans. And now, everybody is coming from all the world from Vilna. The written of them is here was buried 75,000 Jewish people from Vilna. This is the rings what was marry in ghetto in 1945. And it was written inset in the ring. They died and they died in the year. And then I took this with me to the concentration camp. And I hide them under a tongue and bring them back. I bring them back, and my husband bring them back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what date were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 17th of-- the 17th of April, 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mrs. Drabkin. Thank you for the interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
